does: This paper introduces FRIT, a training method that improves the causal faithfulness of chain-of-thought reasoning in language models. The core contribution is a scalable alignment approach that generates synthetic training data by systematically intervening on individual reasoning steps in model-generated chains of thought, creating pairs of examples that highlight when reasoning breaks down versus when it remains causally consistent. The method then applies Direct Preference Optimization to teach models to prefer causally grounded reasoning paths. Evaluated on Qwen3-8B and Mistral-7B-v0.1 across factual and symbolic reasoning tasks including GSM8K, FRIT demonstrates improvements in faithful reasoning (3.4 percentage points for Mistral) and overall task accuracy (7.6 percent improvement), showing that explicit training on causal consistency can make model reasoning more trustworthy and aligned with actual problem-solving logic.
connects: This work responds to growing evidence that chain-of-thought prompting, while effective for improving performance, often produces reasoning steps that do not causally influence final answers. It builds on prior work measuring faithfulness in language model reasoning but addresses the limitation that existing approaches focus mainly on evaluation rather than systematic improvement. The paper combines causal intervention methodology from interpretability research with preference learning and alignment techniques like Direct Preference Optimization, connecting threads from causal inference, mechanistic interpretability of neural networks, and RLHF-style alignment methods. It extends earlier work on understanding when and why reasoning explanations fail to guide model behavior, adding a constructive improvement method rather than just diagnostic measurement.
enables: This work opens directions for building more trustworthy and interpretable language models by establishing that causal consistency in reasoning can be systematically improved through targeted training. The synthetic data generation approach via causal intervention could extend to other forms of model behavior beyond chain-of-thought, potentially improving transparency across reasoning domains. It enables future research on scaling faithfulness improvements to larger models and more complex reasoning tasks, combining causal analysis with modern alignment techniques. The method could support downstream applications requiring explainable decision-making, such as scientific discovery or high-stakes reasoning tasks where users need to understand and verify model logic. It also points toward developing stronger connections between interpretability research and practical alignment, suggesting causal understanding can be operationalized in training objectives rather than remaining purely diagnostic.
